steps.title: Identify where punctuation interrupts the verb-object bond
steps.content:
The sentence structure is subject + verb + object: “Florida-based researchers Martha A.

Scholl, Maoya Bassiouni, and Angel J. Torres-Sánchez” (subject) + “compiled” (verb) + “climate data from several sites in Puerto Rico's Luquillo Mountains” (object).

The blank appears right before “compiled,” the transitive verb that must connect directly to the object that follows.

steps.title: Match the choice that preserves the necessary connection
steps.content:
Choice B (“compiled” alone) correctly places the verb to flow directly into its object without any interruption.

Choices A, C, and D all insert punctuation marks—comma, semicolon, and colon respectively—between the verb and its object, fragmenting the grammatical unit that must remain bonded together within the clause.

Standard English requires that a transitive verb and its direct object stay connected without separation.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
